title: War Comes to London Voluntary Hospitals
rights: public domain (presumed; pre-1963 broadcast, no rights statement on archive.org item)
transcript: A warning telegram from the Ministry of Health set the thing in motion. We immediately took steps to restricted missions here to acute cases and to evacuate all patients who were fit to be moved. Sixty students had been trained to act to stretch a batter as to carry the patients and the wards to the waiting ambulances. There were 600 patients in the hospital, and in a few days more than half of these had been taken to their homes by their relatives or by private individuals who volunteered to lend their cars. A hundred and fifty who still required hospital treatment were sent by buses converted into ambulances and then by train to provincial hospital. Our patients were evacuated to every part of the country without a hitch and all we had left was a residue of faulty who were too ill to be moved. Within our building, the top floorboards were clearest. Also, the departments in operating theatres, whose task groups made them unsafe in air raids. But we head ready, our emergency operating theatres at the basement. Forty thousand sandbags were filled and put into position to protect the vital parts of the building. By nearly half of our 5,000 windows were taped for flying glass and darkened for back-out. The last patient goes to Charming O'Lady. The corners of her stretcher were filled with have loins and pastels, and on top of them lay one of the daily papers on which she smilingly pointed out a large headline which read, and a good time was head by all. At the middle of the X, they are bedding radium. There were two very good reasons why we had to do this. In the first place, even if it's present And comparatively low price, radium costs about 200,000 pounds an ounce. We roam in between two and three ounces in London. The second reason, perhaps even more important, will be the effects on life and health of the breaking by high explosives, the hermetically sealed platinum containers, and the dispersal of the radio. It's quite possible, if such an accident occurred, the surrounding neighbourhood will become inhabitable. With these facts in mind, and some months ago, warholes were sunk at a few selected hospitals, and special containers and hoisting gear were made. These arrangements were so complete that the whole of the Radium in London was 50 feet below ground of 24 hours notice. This was, of course, only a temporary solution, as we couldn't contemplate stopping more radium treatment for the duration of the world. More boreholes are now being sunk, so that the individual hospitals can safeguard their own radium and arrangements have been made to carry on treatment in the data and put the radium underground at night. If radium is being used when an air raid alarms soundly, it can move and put in a borehole within a few minutes. All this increased handling of radium has raised the question of the protection of hospitals starch from the names of their health spray radiation. Some of the hospitals have simplified this protection question by having the members of the staff carry a tiny instrument in their pockets, which records the accumulated amount of radiation received. On the whole, we think we know a scheme whereby the rating stocks are fully protected, and at the same time cancer and other treatments can still be carried out. Within ten minutes of the warning received on the telephone, all the anti-sprinter, self-blinds are drawn on the windows in the heavily sandbaged hospital, and our staff wheeled the 50 or 60 beds containing mothers into the safe corridors and give each of them their mask and a cup of tea. Meanwhile, the babies have been moving to large gas refuge rooms, our voters are standing by as firemen. When the queen came here the other day, she said how pleased she was to see the electric arrangements in our corridors for making tea for the patients and staff during an air raid. Many of our mothers are wives of servicemen, the traditional hair since Defalga. Everyone is very cheerful as they knit away for the soldiers during the air raid alarms. I am a member of the surgical staff of St. Pothole News. And part of my duty is to treat patients in the outpatients department. Since the outbreak of war, our large outpatients department have been transferred to the basement of a new steel and concrete building, which has been further protected with some 100,000 sandbags filled and placed in position by the medical students and the permanent staff. In our new quarters, we are equipped as efficiently as in peacetime for the treatment of patients. The wards, X-ray department, operating theaters, and other special departments are near at hand. The space available may be rather smaller, but it is much safer and furthermore, it is so arranged as to enable us quickly to dispose and treat any sudden rush of raid casualties. Barts has worked for over 800 years through many wars. Some of them just as vital to the nation's existence as a present country. And the hospital will continue to every hour of the day and night to be available for the treatment of the sick and suffering part. When the war came, it scattered the patient students and staff of guys all over the county of Kent. And so we were rarely divided into five parts. Guys itself and four hostels between the Thames and the Midway. But such is the vitality of the school, and so much of its spirit is carried by the students to their new homes that the teaching of medicine was hardly interrupted at all. In those first days, we had to work under great difficulty. And guys, students who were working day and night digging trenches, filling sandbags and carrying stretchers still found time to attend lectures. The teachers had to manage to fit in these lectures as they were also fully occupied in reorganising the hospital services on a wartime basis. Now the hospital is filling many of its empty beds. Its outpatients are once more becoming crowded and the 150 students still left the guys, follow a program of work at least as full as before the war. In the country, the difficulties were even greater. For here, the staff and students were permitted more or less where they could find room. The new hospitals were not equipped for teaching, but with the willing help of the her or the medical staff of the county hospitals, lecture, theatres and classrooms were improvised And now the teaching of the 150 clinical students in the country is as energetic and full as of the parent hospital. We thought it better to keep in London only those students who had hospital duties before. The original idea was to send the others to Oxford. But since the clinical school was in London and Kent, it was decided to keep the pre-medical and the dental school in Kent, too. Special trains and hoplaries were used to carry the teachers and students with all their elaborate technical apparatus through several large country houses near Tumberidge Wells. There, too, the new conditions did not interfere with the students' industry. The students made possible all the work which had been done, and the way they adapted themselves to difficult conditions was remarkable.
